text: The surgeon begins dissection using a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a monopolar hook in the right hand, attempting to find a plane between the inferior aspect of the liver and the omentum. The dissection involves swiping movements for blunt dissection and short bursts of energy to carefully separate the tissues without causing injury.

The surgeon creates a dissection plane inferiorly, staying close to the gallbladder while pushing the surrounding fat downward with steady traction. The duodenum is unexpectedly positioned high, resulting in a cautery injury that requires repair to prevent leakage.
The surgeon clears the omentum from the area around the gallbladder fundus, revealing a small knuckle of duodenum attached near the fundus, indicative of a cholecystoid duodenal fistula.

The surgeon repairs the area of duodenum that received cautery injury using a 3-0 PDS suture and a needle driver. Interrupted stitches are placed to bury the damaged tissue, ensuring it heals properly without leakage.

The surgeon continues dissection around the fistulous tract extending from the duodenum to the fundus of the gallbladder, using a robotic clip applicator for retraction. The Firefly mode is deployed to delineate the biliary tree, revealing the obstruction at the gallbladder neck and visualization on the duodenum side. An articulating SureForm stapler from the Da Vinci system is visible.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to create a three-row staple line, leaving it without reinforcement stitches. A plane is created further down, revealing thickened and vascular tissue due to chronic inflammation, causing slight oozing. The surgeon attempts to identify the cystic artery, cystic duct, and lymph node of the lung as anatomical landmarks, using firefly mode for guidance. Despite no delineation, the surgeon enters the gallbladder, resulting in mucus discharge.

The surgeon performs further dissection around the gallbladder, preparing for a subtotal cholecystectomy. The neck of the gallbladder is transected to avoid injury to nearby structures such as the common bile duct, hepatic artery, or portal vein. Closure is performed at the neck region to conclude the procedure safely.

The surgeon clears debris from the neck region and cystic duct junction using suction irrigation. A stump is created, and a V-lock or barb suture is used to close the stump. The surgeon demonstrates closing the stump with running stitches in a vertical fashion, especially when the tissue is friable, edematous, or inflamed.

The surgeon performs the final steps of the surgery by dissecting the gallbladder remnant from the liver bed. The planes are fused, requiring careful dissection rather than simple lifting. The surgeon uses short bursts of electrocautery and constant traction with the left hand to open the plane in the scarred area. The dissection is carried out from multiple angles, including top to bottom and left to right, utilizing the maneuverability of the robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to detach the final piece of tissue connecting the gallbladder to the liver bed, completing the cholecystectomy procedure.
